segments.s066-g121: چنان is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' and modifies the following verb. دید is the simple past 3rd-person singular of دیدن ('he saw'). رودابه را is the direct object marked by the enclitic را (definite/object marker); the name precedes را in standard Persian word order. در نهان is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('in secret / secretly') giving the location of the seeing.
segments.s066-g122: کجا here means 'where' (introducing a location clause). نشنود is the negative form (ن + شنود) expressing that someone does not (or would not) hear — in epic/poetic narrative it carries a potential/irrealis sense 'would not hear'. پند کس literally 'the advice of anyone' (پند = advice; کس = anyone/someone) and functions as the object of نشنود. در جهان ('in the world') is a locative phrase narrowing the domain — 'in the whole world.' The clause as a whole qualifies the preceding 'in secret' by saying it was in a place where nobody in the world could hear counsel.
segments.s066-g123: بیامد is the past tense 'he came/arrived.' ز تیمار ('from care/anguish') is an ablative phrase giving the cause or source. گریان is a participial/adjunct form 'weeping,' describing the manner of his coming. بخفت is past tense of خفتن ('he slept' / 'he lay down'), placed after the participial phrase to continue the action sequence: 'he came weeping and slept.'
segments.s066-g124: همی is an adverb here meaning 'still' / 'even then' and gives an ongoing/emphatic flavor. پوست بر تنش is a noun phrase 'skin on his body' where بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and ش is the enclitic possessive 'his' attached to تن. گفتی functions idiomatically as an impersonal or modal poetic particle meaning 'one would say' or 'it may be said' (common in classical Persian narrative), not the literal noun 'conversation.' بکفت is an archaic/past resultative meaning 'it was enough' (i.e. 'would suffice'); together the clause means 'his skin clung to his body — one would say that was enough.'
segments.s066-g97: This is a short clause in the passive: فرستاده شد = “a messenger was sent.” نزد + proper name places the action in someone's presence (‘to the house/side of’). بزرگ follows the name as an epithet ‘the great/elder Sam.’ Word order is noun + passive auxiliary + preposition + name + epithet.
segments.s066-g98: Here the verb فرستاد (past of فرستادن) is active: (he) sent. The implied subject is Sam (carried over from the previous clause). پاسخ is the object ‘a reply,’ and به introduces the recipient (زال). This is a terse, classical style where the subject is omitted because it is clear from context.
segments.s066-g99: زمانی is a temporal adverb ‘at that time/after a while.’ بپیچید is a past tense form of پیچیدن meaning ‘turned’ or ‘changed direction/turned away’ (poetic/older narrative past). و دستور بود is ‘and there was an order’ — دستور is a noun ‘order/command’ and بود is the past copula indicating existence (‘there was’).
segments.s066-g100: سخنهای (literally ‘words/speeches’) is pluralized and modified by the adjective بایسته ‘necessary/proper.’ گفت و شنود is a terse, classical coordination: گفت (said) and شنود (an archaic/poetic form meaning ‘heard’). Together they mean that the fitting words were said and listened to; the verbs are past narrative forms.
segments.s066-g101: فرستاده را uses the object-marker را to mark ‘the messenger’ as the indirect object/recipient, and داد is the past verb ‘gave’ — so the clause means ‘(he) gave the messenger many things.’ بسیار چیز is the quantifier + noun ‘many things’ (بسیار here = ‘many’).
segments.s066-g102: شنیدم is first‑person past ‘I heard.’ همه modifies پاسخ: ‘all (of) the reply,’ سام is the possessor (Sam’s reply), and نیز adds ‘also/too.’ The clause states the narrator heard the whole response as well.
segments.s066-g103: به دست همین زن = ‘by the hand of this very woman’ (به + دست indicates agency/means). که introduces a relative clause ‘who…’. کندیش is the verb کند (to do/pull/pluck) with the enclitic pronoun -ش (meaning ‘it’/‘his’/‘her’) attached, so ‘who plucked it’; موی is the object ‘hair’ and completes the relative clause. This is classical syntax with enclitic object pronouns.
segments.s066-g104: زدی is past of زدن ‘you/he struck’ and takes بر زمین ‘on the ground’ as the place of the action. و links the second verb کشیدی (past ‘you pulled/dragged’). به روی = ‘onto/over the face’ (روی = face). Both verbs are past narrative and the objects/pronouns are omitted because they were mentioned earlier (the hair).
segments.s066-g41: بدین حجره = a prepositional phrase meaning 'for/to this chamber' (بدین is an older/poetic form meaning 'to/for this'); رودابه is the proper name; پیرایه is the noun 'decoration/ornament' that she desired; خواست is a past-tense third-person singular verb ('wanted'/'asked for'), so the clause reads 'Rudabeh wanted ornamentation for this chamber.'
segments.s066-g42: بدو دادم = 'I gave (it) to her' with بدو 'to her' (prepositional/indirect-object form) preceding the verb; دادم is first-person past 'I gave'. اکنون همینست راست is a parenthetical comment: اکنون 'now', همینست 'this is it/that is all', راست 'true/indeed' — an emphatic assertion that this is indeed the case now.
segments.s066-g43: بیاوردمش is a past-tense verb 'I brought/fetched' with the enclitic object -ش ('her/it'); افسر is 'helmet' (here the object brought); پرنگار is a compound adjective 'richly ornamented' modifying افسر, placed after the noun in poetic word order.
segments.s066-g44: یکی حلقه = 'one/a ring' (یکی as the numeral/indefinite 'one' or 'a certain'); پرگوهر 'full of gems/jeweled' and شاهوار 'regal/lofty' are adjectival modifiers following حلقه, so the phrase describes a single, precious, kingly ring.
segments.s066-g45: بدو گفت = 'he said to her/to him' (indirect object phrase before the verb, common in Persian); سیندخت is the vocative/proper name; بنمایی‌ام is the verb form 'show me' (بنمایی = present/subjunctive/imperative sense 'you show', plus -ام 'me' as the object), so together 'Sindokht, show me' or 'will you show yourself to me?'.
segments.s066-g46: دل‌بسته = 'enamored/smitten' (predicate describing the speaker); ز اندیشه = 'from/of (this) thought' (ز = 'from' in poetic register, اندیشه = 'thought'); بگشایی‌ام is 'you would/unlock/release me' (بگشایی = present/subjunctive or optative 'you loosen/open', + -ام 'me'), so the clause asks/claims that she will free him from anxious thought because he is love-struck.
segments.s066-g47: سپردم به رودابه = 'I entrusted/handed over (them) to Rudabeh' (سپردم first-person past 'I entrusted', به + proper name marks the recipient); گفت این دو چیز = '(she) said: these two things' (این دو = 'these two', چیز = 'things/items'), introducing what Rudabeh requested.
segments.s066-g48: فزون خواست = 'wanted more/extra' (فزون here has the sense 'additional/more'); اکنون بیارمش نیز = اکنون 'now', بیارمش 'bring them/him/her' (بیارم + -ش object clitic), نیز 'also/too' — so the speaker is asked to bring those additional two items now as well.
segments.s066-g17: به نزدیک رودابه آمد is a prepositional phrase + verb: به + نزدیک + رودابه = 'came near Rudabeh' (preposition before the adjective/place). آمد is past tense 3rd-singular 'he came/approached'. چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = 'like/as', باد = 'wind') that specifies the manner.
segments.s066-g18: بدین شادمانی ورا مژده داد: بدین here functions as be+این ('with/by this' or 'to this') introducing the manner/content ('with this joy'); ورا is the enclitic pronoun 'to her', مژده 'good news', and داد is simple past 'gave/announced'. Word order is prepositional phrase + indirect object + direct noun + verb.
segments.s066-g19: پری روی is a compound describing appearance ('fairy-faced' or 'of fairy-like face'; پری used adjectivally). The verb phrase بر ... برفشاند is transitive: بر + noun (بر زن درم = 'dirhams upon the woman') followed by برفشاند 'scattered/strewn' (past 3rd-singular). Word order places the prepositional object before the verb, a common poetic/orderly inversion.
segments.s066-g20: به کرسی زر is a prepositional phrase 'onto a golden seat/throne' (به = to/onto, کرسی = seat/throne, زر = gold). پیکرش is the object 'her body/figure' (note the possessive suffix), and برنشاند is a compound verb 'placed/caused to sit on' (بر- + نشاند), past tense 3rd-singular acting on پیکرش.
segments.s066-g21: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain (one)'. شاره is a rare/poetic descriptor for the object (here left as an uncertain epithet in the text). سربند 'headband' is the direct object. پیش آورید is the verbal compound 'bring forward' (پیش آوردن); here the form is poetic/archaic (آورید rather than the expected modern آورَد/آورد) functioning as 'brought forward'.
segments.s066-g22: شده is the perfect/passive-like 'has become' or 'was made'. تار و پود 'warp and weft' names the fabric structure; اندرو is an archaic/poetic compound meaning 'in it/within', and ناپدید is 'vanished/invisible'. The clause reads 'its warp and weft inside have become invisible.'
segments.s066-g23: همه پیکرش = 'her whole/entire body' (همه quantifying پیکرش). سرخ یاقوت و زر places two color/material predicates ('red as ruby and gold') that describe the body; the actual copula/predicate 'شده' appears in the next segment, so the adjectives follow the noun in poetic inversion.
segments.s066-g24: شده continues the predication 'has become' (linking to the preceding adjectival phrase). زر همه ('all the gold') is the subject/quantity that follows, ناپدید 'vanished' is the resulting state, and از گهر 'from the jewel' indicates the source/place from which the gold disappeared. The clause reads 'all the gold had vanished from the jewel.'
segments.s066-g89: سپهدار (sepahdār) is a title 'commander' placed before its modifier; دستان here functions as the name/epithet attached to the commander (classical word order: Title + name). به کابل بماند is a past-subjunctive/optative or narrative-perfect sense of ماندن 'to remain/stay' with به marking the destination/place 'in/at Kabul' — literally 'stayed at Kabul.'
segments.s066-g90: چنین is a demonstrative/adverb 'thus/so.' مهر here means a kind of ‘seal/affection’ and functions as the direct object; اویم is an archaic possessive/relational form built on او, indicating 'his/of him' (classical Persian morphology), so مهر اویم = 'his seal/affection.' بر آتش نشاند uses the transitive past stem of نشانیدن (نشاند) meaning 'placed/laid/caused to sit,' with بر آتش 'upon the fire' as the spatial complement: 'he placed (it) upon the fire.'
segments.s066-g91: چنان تنگ شد is a copular-construction: چنان 'so' + تنگ 'tight/distressing' + شد 'became' = 'became so tight/distressing.' بر دلم is a prepositional phrase 'on my heart' (دل + possessive ـم), and بر جهان repeats the locative 'on the world/this life' — classical Persian often stacks locatives for emphasis.
segments.s066-g92: که here introduces the result clause 'so that/that': گریان شدم is the past tense 'I became weeping' (shodam = I became/I was made to be weeping). آشکار و نهان are adverbial/adjuncts 'openly and secretly' stacked for contrast ('both publicly and in private').
segments.s066-g93: نخواهم is the classical negative of خواستن in a hortative/optative sense 'I would not want/I do not wish.' بدن زنده is the noun phrase 'a living body' (بدن 'body' + زنده 'alive'). بی‌روی او is a phrase 'without face/of no beauty (without her presence/face)' with او as the referent; the whole means 'a living body bereft of her visage.'
segments.s066-g94: جهانم is جهان 'world/this life' with the possessive suffix ـم 'my' (my life/world). نیرزد is an impersonal/present negative form meaning 'is not worth / does not deserve.' به یک موی او is the comparison phrase 'to a single hair of hers' (به marks the standard of comparison). The clause states that the speaker’s entire world is not worth one hair of the beloved.
segments.s066-g95: بدان کو is a classical relative/demonstrative cluster meaning 'to that one who' (بِدان/بدان + کو 'who/which'); مرا دید is the object-marker phrase 'saw me' (مرا = me as object, دید = saw). و بامن نشست is 'and sat with me' (بامن = with me, نشست = sat). Word order is classical: relative phrase → object → verb; verbs at the end of the clause.
segments.s066-g96: به پیمان گرفتیم is a past 1st‑person plural 'we took (a) covenant/pledge' (گرفتیم = we took/accepted, به پیمان = 'into covenant'). دستش بدست is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'hand in his hand' / 'hand to hand' (دست + ـش = his hand, بدست = 'in/into the hand' used adverbially): i.e. they took his hand into theirs as part of the pledge.
segments.s066-g105: فرستاده آرنده نامه بود is a past‑tense copular clause: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' and آرنده is a present‑participle/agent noun 'the one who brought', forming 'the messenger who brought (the) letter'. نامه is the object 'letter'. بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order (noun + participle + object + بود) is poetic but yields 'he was the messenger who brought the letter.'
segments.s066-g106: مرا پاسخ‌نامه این جامه بود literally reads 'to me the reply‑letter this garment was.' مرا is the dative/accusative 'to me' (here marking the person for whom the reply is intended), پاسخ‌نامه 'reply/answer‑letter' is the predicate noun, این 'this' specifies جامه 'garment/robe', and بود is the past copula 'was'. The sense is 'this garment was the reply to me.'
segments.s066-g107: فروماند سیندخت زان گفت‌گوی: فروماند is a past/state verb meaning 'was left/was left behind/overcome' (poetic usage), followed by the proper name سیندخت as the affected person. زان ('from that') plus گفت‌گوی ('that talk/conversation') gives the cause: 'by/after that conversation.' The verb precedes the subject here, a word order frequently used in verse.
segments.s066-g108: پسند آمدش زال را جفت اوی is an idiomatic construction. پسند آمدش ('it pleased him' / 'he liked her') uses آمد (here آمدش = آمد + enclitic pronoun ش = 'to him/her'). زال را marks Zal as the experiencer/indirect object ('to Zal'), and جفت اوی ('she was a match for him') is the predicate: جفت 'mate/spouse' with اوی as the third‑person pronoun referring to the woman. Together: 'Zal liked her; she was his match.'
segments.s066-g109: چنین داد پاسخ که این خرد نیست: such that 'چنین' ('thus') + داد پاسخ ('gave an answer') introduces the quoted content with که. The clause این خرد نیست ('this is not wisdom') is a straightforward copular negative: این 'this' + خرد 'wisdom' + نیست 'is not.' داد here is the verb 'gave' (he gave an answer).
segments.s066-g110: چو دستان ز پرمایگان گرد نیست: چو here functions causal/conditional 'since/as' (linking reason). دستان in poetic usage is best read as 'tales/stories' (not literal hands) and ز + پرمایگان 'from/of the nobles' gives the source/group. گرد نیست is a negative predication meaning 'is not among/does not belong to' (i.e. 'such tales are not the way of the nobles'). The whole clause gives the justification for calling it 'not wisdom.'
segments.s066-g111: بزرگست پور جهان‌پهلوان places the predicate 'بزرگست' ('is great') before the noun phrase 'پور جهان‌پهلوان' ('the son of the world‑champion'). پور is 'son/descendant' and جهان‌پهلوان is a compound noun 'world‑champion' (a mighty hero). The phrase means 'the son of the world‑champion is great.'
segments.s066-g112: همش نام و هم رای روشن‌روان: همش 'all of it/entirely' modifies the following nouns. نام here is 'name' in the sense of 'reputation', و 'and', هم رای 'and likewise every counsel/opinion' — رای in that context means 'counsel/opinion'. روشن‌روان is an adjectival compound 'bright‑soul(ed)' applied to both 'name' and 'rāy', so the line says his whole reputation and his every counsel are of a bright/clear spirit.
segments.s066-g113: هنرها همه هست is literal word order (noun + quantifier + copula) meaning “all the arts exist / he has every art”; همه after the noun emphasizes totality. The second phrase آهو یکی omits an explicit copula (is) and apposes آهو as ‘one (of those)’ — here آهو is a poetic epithet (‘the gazelle’ / beloved).
segments.s066-g114: که introduces a consequence/result clause (“such that / so that”). گردد (from گردیدن/گشتن) here means “become” (classical narration/aorist sense). هنر پیش او اندکی is a compact phrase: هنر = skill(s), پیش او = in her presence / before her (پیش = ‘in the presence of’), اندکی = small/a little — together: “skill becomes small before her.” Word order is literary: verb (گردد) comes before its complement.
segments.s066-g115: شود (شدن) here is the classical narrative/aorist sense “became.” شاه گیتی is a grand title “the king of the world” (noun + epithet). بدین is a fused prepositional/adverbial form meaning “thus / to this degree”; خشمناک is the predicate adjective “angry / wrathful.” The order (verb before subject/adverb) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s066-g116: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” کابل is the source place. برآرد (برآوردن) = “raise / bring forth” (third person). به + خورشید + خاک is a poetic goal phrase: به = “to/up to,” خورشید = “the sun” (literal or figurative splendor), خاک = “dust/earth” (here ‘dust’ fits the image). The verb governs the following object phrase in inverted poetic order.
segments.s066-g117: نخواهد (from خواستن) is a negative modal meaning “will not wish / will not permit.” که introduces the subordinate clause (“that …”). از تخم ما = “from our seed” (i.e., of our descendants). بر زمین = the locative phrase “on the earth,” with بر used in literary/epic sense.
segments.s066-g118: کسی = “anyone / any person” (here under the negation of نخواهد = ‘no one’). پای خوار is an adjectival phrase: پای = “foot” used metonymically for person or rank, خوار = “despised/ignoble.” اندر آرد is a literary order of آوردن with اندر (in/into) following the object; آرد = “brings/sets” (3rd pers.). به زین = “onto the saddle” (زین = saddle). Together: “that no ignoble person of our seed put a despised foot into the saddle.”
segments.s066-g119: رها کرد is a compound verb (رها + کرد) meaning “released/freed” (object follows, marked by را). زن را = “the woman” with definite object marker را. و = “and.” بنواختش (بنواخت + ـش) is a past-form verb with enclitic ـش = ‘her/it’; بنواخت can mean “struck” or “struck a note/played,” and in narrative physical action sense (“struck her”) is likely here.
segments.s066-g120: چنان کرد پیدا is a formulaic sequence: چنان = ‘so/thus,’ کرد = ‘made/did,’ پیدا = ‘visible/manifest’ (پیدا کردن = make appear). The following که clause gives the result: که = ‘that,’ نشناختش = negative past of شناختن with enclitic ـش = ‘he did not recognize her.’ So: “He made her so manifest that he did not recognize her.”
segments.s066-g65: دو گل را is the direct object: دو گل (two flowers) followed by the object marker را making it definite/specific. به دو نرگس خوابدار uses به in an instrumental/agent-like sense ('with/by means of') and places the modifier خوابدار ('sleepy') after the noun نرگس, the normal adjective order in Persian poetry.
segments.s066-g66: همی شست combines the particle همی (a Classical/poetic continuous/habitual marker) with the past form شست 'washed' to give an ongoing/past action ('was washing' or 'washed repeatedly'). تا شد introduces a result clause 'until/so that they became,' with شد 'became' as the intransitive predicative verb and گلان آبدار the subject + predicate adjective ('the blossoms/dews became moist').
segments.s066-g67: به رودابه گفت is a prepositional indirect-object construction: به + name = 'to Rudabeh' and گفت is the main past verb 'said' introducing the following direct address. ای سرافراز ماه is a vocative phrase (ای + epithet + noun) addressing her as 'O proud/lofty moon' (a poetic epithet).
segments.s066-g68: گزین کردی is a two-word verbal phrase: گزین (choose) + کردی (past 2nd-singular auxiliary) = 'did you choose'. از ناز برگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the court’s coquetry/charming ways' (از + noun phrase), and چاه is the direct object placed at the end ('the well/pit'), giving the sense 'did you choose the well from the court's coquettishness?'.
segments.s066-g69: چه ماند is a rhetorical question 'what remained/was left'. از introduces the source/partitive relation 'of/from the good (نکو) you had' where نکو ('good') is linked to داشتی ('you had') — داشتی is the 2nd-person past 'you had'. در جهان ('in the world') is a locative phrase modifying 'you had'.
segments.s066-g70: که introduces the subordinate clause explaining the preceding question. ننمودمت is negative + verbal form meaning 'I did not show/reveal you' with the clitic -مت marking 'to you' (object clitic). آشکار و نهان are paired adverbial/predicative words 'openly and secretly' specifying the manner in which something was (not) shown.
segments.s066-g71: This is a vocative interrogative: ستمگر چرا گشتی is 'Why did you become an oppressor/tyrant?' with چرا 'why' and گشتی (past 'you became') addressing the addressee. ای/implicit vocative ماه‌روی 'moon‑faced one' follows as the poetic address. Word order is compressed in poetry but reads as 'Why have you become cruel, O moon-faced one?'.
segments.s066-g72: همه رازها is the direct object 'all the secrets.' پیش مادر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of/to the mother' (i.e. 'to your mother'), and بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form ('say!/tell!') addressed to the same addressee; in Classical Persian بگوی is used for 'tell' (command/request).
segments.s066-g49: This clause starts with an agent/name (بها) followed by گفت (past of گفتن 'said'); بگذار is an imperative/subjunctive meaning 'let/allow' and takes the following prepositional phrase بر چشم من ('upon my eye') as its complement. Word order is Subject (name) + گفت (said) + quoted/imperative clause; چشم من is a noun + possessive pronoun ('my eye') where من follows the noun as classical Persian possessive.
segments.s066-g50: یکی آب functions as 'one (drop/measure) of water' (numeric determiner + noun). بر + verb زن is an instructive sequence where زن is the imperative of زدن ('strike/pour' here), so آب بر زن = 'pour/strike water (on)'. برین is orthographically 'بر این' ('on/against this'), conditioning the following noun خشم من ('my anger'); the final من is the possessive 'my'.
segments.s066-g51: درم is a direct object noun 'dirham' (a coin); گفت here is the reporting verb 'said' introducing the reported instruction. فردا دهد is a temporal phrase + present/future-tense verb 'will give' (دهد as medial/future-like form used in narration). ماه روی is a compound meaning 'moon-faced' (lit. 'moon face') — ماه used poetically for a beautiful person and روی = 'face'.
segments.s066-g52: This is an address + subordinate temporal clause. بها is vocative (the addressee's name here); تا introduces the temporal subordinate 'until'. نیابم is the subjunctive/imperfective form of یافتن/یابیدن used here as 'I (may) find', and مجوی is an imperative negative 'do not seek' addressed to تو ('you'); از من marks 'from me' (i.e. do not seek me).
segments.s066-g53: همی is an adverbial particle intensifying the verb (here 'regarded/considered'); کژ is an adjective 'crooked/wrong' modifying گفتار ('speech'), and دانست is the past tense of دانستن used in the sense 'deemed/considered'. The order is adverb + adjective + verb + noun phrase (object): 'he indeed considered his speech crooked.'
segments.s066-g54: بیاراست is a verb meaning 'set in order/prepare' (here 'made ready'); دل را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'the heart' as the object. به + پیکار او means 'for/against his battle' — i.e. 'prepared his heart for fighting him.' Word order: verb + object (marked by را) + prepositional phrase of purpose/target.
segments.s066-g55: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. بجستش is a preterite form meaning 'he sprang/ leapt at him' (the suffixed -ش marks 'him'). بر is the preposition 'upon/at' reinforcing the motion onto the other, and و + آستی links with slight manner adverbial sense; آستی here conveys manner/tone (often 'gently/quickly' depending on context). The sequence describes motion toward and attack on the addressee.
segments.s066-g56: همی is an intensifier ('indeed/still'); جست is another past form of 'leap/spring' (here 'he leapt'), and ازو = 'from him' indicates origin or target in older usage. کژی و کاستی are nouns 'crookedness and lack/shortcoming' listing the faults motivating the action. The clause thus says he leapt at him concerning those faults.
segments.s066-g1: This is a prepositional phrase with the preposition میان ('between') placed before its two coordinated nouns. آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying سرو ('cypress'), and بن is a post‑noun element meaning 'base' or 'trunk' (so 'that cypress‑trunk'). Word order is typical classical Persian: preposition + noun phrase.
segments.s066-g2: زنی بود is an existential clause in the past tense: 'there was a woman.' The predicate that further describes her follows: گوینده شیرین سخن ('a speaker of sweet speech'). شیرین modifies سخن ('speech'), forming a descriptive noun phrase; گوینده is a nominal predicate (or apposition) linked by بود.
segments.s066-g3: پیام is the direct object 'a message'; آوریدی is an archaic/past second‑person form 'you brought' (past tense, 2nd sg.). سوی پهلوان is the prepositional phrase 'to/toward the champion' that takes the action of آوردی.
segments.s066-g4: هم functions as 'also/likewise' linking this clause to the previous. از پهلوان indicates origin 'from the champion', سوی سرو indicates destination 'toward the cypress', and روان here has the sense of 'proceeding' or 'moving' (a predicate describing motion, continuing the subject 'you' from the previous clause). The verb is verbless in form but روان carries the verbal/adjectival meaning.
segments.s066-g5: This clause has سپهدار as subject. دستان مر is a possessive/genitive expression 'my tales' (دستان here = 'tales/stories' rather than literal 'hands', with مر an older form of the first‑person marker), او را is the indirect object 'to her' marked by the object marker را, and بخواند is a past tense verb used here to mean 'recited' or 'read aloud.' Word order places the possessed noun before the verb, with the indirect object marked by را.
segments.s066-g6: سخن هر چه بشنید is a fronted nominal clause meaning 'whatever words he had heard' (هر چه + verbal past بشنید = 'whatever he heard'). با او is 'to/with her' and modifies the following verb. براند is used poetically from راندن; here it functions as 'uttered/repeated to' (i.e., he put forth those words to her). The clause as a whole says he repeated to her every word he had heard.
segments.s066-g7: بدو is an enclitic form meaning 'to him' (equivalent to به او) and گفْت is the past 'he said'. نزدیک رودابه is the directive 'near Rudabeh/close to Rudabeh'. رو here functions as an instruction 'go/take your face toward' (imperative sense 'approach'), a concise classical usage meaning 'go toward' or 'present yourself before'.
segments.s066-g8: بگویش is the imperative 'tell/say to her' (بگو + enclitic ش = 'to her'). که introduces the reported/direct speech ('say that:'), and the following sequence is a vocative address: ای (vocative) + نیک‌دل ('kind‑hearted') followed by ماه نو ('new moon'), a poetic epithet of beauty. The whole segment is the content the messenger is ordered to deliver.
segments.s066-g57: 'به خشم اندرون شد' is a verbal phrase meaning 'entered into anger / became angry': 'به' (towards/into) + 'خشم' (anger) + 'اندرون' (within) precede the past-tense copula 'شد' (= 'became'). Classical Persian often places the subject after the verb, so 'ازان زن' ('that woman') follows and is the subject; 'غمی' ('grief') is a predicative/complement indicating her state. Overall: 'That woman became inwardly angry and grieved.'
segments.s066-g58: 'به خواری کشیدش' uses 'به' as a manner/adverbial marker ('in/with humiliation') and the verb 'کشید' in past tense plus the enclitic object pronoun '-ش' ('her' or 'it') to mean 'she endured it.' The final phrase 'بروی زمی' is literal/archaic: 'بروی' (a form of 'to go/come on') with 'زمی' ('earthly/on earth'), giving a sense 'in this world / on earth.'
segments.s066-g59: The clause begins with 'چو' = 'when', followed by the demonstrative 'آن' + noun 'جامه‌های گرانمایه' ('those precious garments'); the past verb 'دید' ('saw') closes the clause. Word order is typical classical Persian (temporal conjunction + topic + verb at end).
segments.s066-g60: 'هم' ('also') starts the clause; 'از دست' is a prepositional phrase 'from the hand/of the hand' and 'رودابه پیرایه' ('Rudabeh [saw] the ornament') places the object 'پیرایه' before the verb 'دید' (past 'saw'). In context the line means Rudabeh also saw the ornament come off/her ornament fall from her hand; classical Persian readily permits this object-before-verb order.
segments.s066-g61: 'در کاخ' is the locative 'in the palace'. The verb phrase 'بر خویشتن بر ببست' literally 'bound/closed herself upon herself' uses the preposition/prefix 'بر' twice with the reflexive noun 'خویشتن' ('herself') and the past verb 'ببست' ('closed/tied'), i.e. she wrapped or shut herself away. Subject is implied (Rudabeh) and appears after the verb in classical order.
segments.s066-g62: 'از اندیشگان شد' = 'she was separated from / ceased to be among the thoughtful' (literally 'from the thoughtful she became'), where 'از' marks separation or source and 'شد' is the past 'became.' The following prepositional/complement 'به کردار مست' ('in behavior like a drunk') describes the result: 'کردار' (conduct) + adjective 'مست' (drunk, used figuratively).
segments.s066-g63: 'بفرمود' here is a polite/honorific past-form verb 'he ordered/said'; it introduces a subordinate purpose/command clause with 'تا' = 'so that/that'. 'دخترش' ('his daughter') is the subject and 'رفت' ('went') the past-tense verb; 'پیش' 'before/in the presence (of him)' completes the clause.
segments.s066-g64: 'همی' is a classical particle indicating a continuing or simple narrative action ('then/she proceeded to'), 'دست برزد' is an archaic verbal phrase meaning she struck/put her hand (here 'laid her hand') — the verb 'برزد' is an older form — and 'به رخسار خویش' is a prepositional phrase 'to/on her own cheek', where 'خوی'+'ش' together form the possessive 'her own'. The sequence yields 'she then laid her hand upon her cheek.'
segments.s066-g25: This is a compact noun phrase. یکی جفت = 'one/a single pair' (یکی preposes the numeral/quantifier). پر + مایه functions as a compound adjective, with پر here meaning 'full of' and مایه 'substance/resources' → 'richly endowed (with gems)'; انگشتری is the head noun 'ring(s)'. Together: 'a single pair of rings, full of (precious) substance.'
segments.s066-g26: فروزنده is a present-participial/adjectival form meaning 'shining/illuminating'. چون is the comparative 'like'. بر + فلک = 'upon the sky/firmament' and مشتری is the simile referent 'Jupiter'. Word order is poetic: adjective + simile phrase → 'shining like Jupiter on the firmament.'
segments.s066-g27: فرستاد is the past tense 'sent'. نزدیک here works adverbially/locatively with دستان سام = 'the hands of Sam' (دستان = hands), so the phrase means 'sent (it) to/into Sam’s hands.' The sequence is subject/verb then an indirect-object locative phrase.
segments.s066-g28: بسی is an adverbial/intensifier meaning 'much' or 'many'; داد is the past 'gave'. با آن introduces what is given/taken along with it: 'with that, (he) gave in greeting and message' — درود و پیام are the items given ('greeting(s) and a message'). Word order places the adverb before the verb and the compound object after it.
segments.s066-g29: Simple motion clause: زن (subject) + از حجره 'from the chamber' (direction/source) + آنگه 'then' + به ایوان رسید 'reached the porch' (رسید = past 'arrived/arrived at'). Word order is subject → source → temporal → destination → verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s066-g30: نگه here is a noun 'a look/stop' used with the light verb کرد to mean 'paused/paused to look'. The next clause names the perceiver: سیندخت (proper name) + او را (accusative 'her') + بدید 'saw'. So syntactically: '(she) paused; Siandokht saw her.' The object marker را marks او as definite/direct object.
segments.s066-g31: زن (subject) + از بیم = 'out of fear' (از introduces the cause/reason) + برگشت 'turned back/returned' (past). چـون here introduces the reason/clause that follows ('because'/'for'), linking to سندروس (sandalwood, a fragrant wood). So: 'The woman turned back out of fear, for (because of) the sandalwood.'
segments.s066-g32: بترسید is a past-tense verb 'she became afraid/feared'. و links the next action. روی زمین = 'upon the ground' (روی = 'on/upon' + زمین = 'ground'); داد بوس is a fixed light-verb expression 'gave (a) kiss' = 'kissed' or 'prostrated (kissed the ground)'. Together: 'she was afraid and kissed the ground.'
segments.s066-g73: This clause opens with the relative/interrogative که (here 'that/who') introducing a question about 'این زن' ('this woman'). ز پیش is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from before / from earlier'. The second که introduces a nested relative clause ('who comes'), and همی is the archaic continuous marker meaning the action is ongoing ('keeps coming'). Word order: relative phrase + source phrase + relative clause with verb at end.
segments.s066-g74: به پیشت is the preposition به plus the pronominal‑locative پیشت ('in your presence / before you'). ز بهر چه literally 'from/for what' asks the purpose ('for what reason / why'); the verb آید (present/subjunctive/epic present) sits before the enclitic continuous همی, again marking repeated/ongoing action. Together the clause asks why she comes into your presence.
segments.s066-g75: سخن ('speech') is the topic; بر چه سانست is an idiomatic old‑style copular question 'in what manner/what sort is (it)' — سانست (= سان + ست) functions as the predicate 'is like/what kind it is' (an archaic formation). The conjunction و links a second question 'آن مرد کیست' ('who is that man?') with the copula ست attached to the interrogative کی (who).
segments.s066-g76: This is a relative clause introduced by که ('who/that') describing 'آن مرد'. زیبای is an adjectival/attributive form 'handsome' modifying the following nouns. سربند و انگشتریست strings two nouns together; the final -ست is the copula attached to انگشتری (here describing the man as 'ring-bearing' or 'wearing a ring'). Word order: adjective before nouns; copula suffixed to the last predicate noun.
segments.s066-g77: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'. گنج بزرگ is a noun phrase 'great treasure'. افسر تازیان is a genitive construction 'the helmet of the Tazians/Arabs' (افسر = helmet). The phrase as a whole lists an item coming from the treasure.
segments.s066-g78: به ما ماند is a result clause: به ما ('to us') + ماند (past/resultant 'remained/was left') — here meaning 'remained to us'. بسیار modifies the following coordinated nouns 'سود و زیان' ('much profit and loss'). Word order places the dative/benefactive phrase before the verb, then the quantity and the coordinated nouns.
segments.s066-g79: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase 'by/under this' (written here as ب + دین); نام بد 'an evil/bad name' is the object. دادخواهی ('seeking justice') is the action being cursed; به باد is an idiom 'to the wind' meaning 'be wasted/vanish' (باد also functions in poetry as an optative/evocative particle). The whole clause expresses an imprecatory wish that the plea for justice under this bad name be blown away.
segments.s066-g80: چو here means 'like/as' (introducing a simile 'a daughter like me'). زاده‌ام is a first‑person formed verbal/adjectival phrase meaning 'born (to me) / of my kind' (here functioning in a wish about a hypothetical birth). دخت 'daughter' is the noun, followed by the negative optative هرگز مباد 'may ... never be' — مباد is an archaic subjunctive/optative particle meaning 'may it not be'.
segments.s066-g9: چون here is the subordinating conjunction 'when/as'. ز is the poetic preposition equivalent to از ('from/of'), so ز تنگی is a prepositional phrase 'from/through narrowness' modifying رسید. رسید is a past simple verb 'reached' (third-person singular). Word order places the topic سخن ('the speech') up front, then the circumstance 'چون ز تنگی' and the verb 'به سختی رسید' = 'reached into difficulty.'
segments.s066-g10: فراخیش is فراخ + ـِش, a noun with the third-person possessive suffix meaning 'its expansiveness/breadth.' را is the definite object marker. زود بینی: بینی is 2nd-person singular present 'you see' (in narrative it often carries a near-future sense, 'you will soon see'); زود 'soon/quickly' modifies it. کلید is a noun placed at the end in apposition/emphasis — here used figuratively ('the key').
segments.s066-g11: فرستاده (messenger) is the subject. باز آمد is a compound meaning 'came back/returned' (باز adverb + آمد). از پیش سام is a prepositional phrase 'from the presence of Sam' (پیش = 'in the presence of' here). The clause is a simple past narrative statement.
segments.s066-g12: ابا here functions as an archaic/poetic prepositional particle equivalent to با ('with') and introduces manner; it is not the lexical 'refusal' sense listed in the provided senses (hence -1). شادمانی و فرخ modify پیام: a coordinated phrase 'with joy and fortunate/auspicious tidings.' پیام is the noun 'tidings/message.'
segments.s066-g13: بسی is an adverb 'much'/'a great deal'. گفت and بشنید are past-tense verbs coordinated by و: 'he spoke and heard/listened.' زد here is a light verb meaning 'to recite/tell' when used with داستان as its object (literally 'struck/told the story'). داستان is the direct object 'the tale/story.'
segments.s066-g14: سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end/finally.' او is the third-person pronoun 'he.' گشت is the past of شدن 'became/turned.' همداستان is a predicate adjective 'like-minded/agreeing' (i.e., 'he came to the same view'). The clause states the eventual result.
segments.s066-g15: سبک پاسخ‌نامه is a noun phrase 'the brief/light reply' (سبک used here in the sense 'light/brief'). پاسخ‌نامه is 'reply/response letter.' زن را marks 'the woman's' (the reply belonging to the woman) with the definite object marker را; سپرد is the past transitive verb 'he handed/entrusted (over)'. The structure indicates that he delivered the woman's short reply.
segments.s066-g16: زن is the subject 'the woman.' از پیش او is the prepositional phrase 'from his presence' (پیش = 'in the presence of'). بازگشت is the past verb 'returned.' و coordinates the next verb; ببرد here means 'took/carry away' (برد in past narrative). The two verbs describe her returning from his presence and taking the reply.
segments.s066-g33: پر اندیشه شد is a predicative phrase: پر ('full (of)') + اندیشه ('thought') together form an adjectival phrase 'full of thought', followed by شد (past tense 'became'). جان سیندخت is the possessor/subject ('Sindokht's life/soul') and ازوی (از + وی) follows as a prepositional/relational phrase meaning 'about/from him' — literally: 'Sindokht's soul became full of thought about him.' Word order is adjective phrase + auxiliary verb + subject + prepositional phrase, typical of classical Persian flexibility.
segments.s066-g34: به آواز گفت is a reporting clause: به آواز ('in a voice', i.e. 'aloud') + گفت (past 'said'). The following clause is direct speech rendered with the subjunctive/imperative form بگوی: از کجایی ('from where') precedes the verb, so از کجایی بگوی = 'say/tell (me) from where (you are)'. بگوی here functions as a jussive/subjunctive/imperative used in classical Persian for a direct request.
segments.s066-g35: زمان تا زمان is a reduplicated adverbial phrase meaning 'from time to time' (occasionally). پیش من بگذری is a present-subjunctive/jussive clause (بگذری) expressing a wish or instruction: 'you pass by/in front of me.' The preposition پیش here means 'in the presence of / before' and modifies the verb phrase.
segments.s066-g36: به حجره درآیی is another jussive/subjunctive: به + حجره ('into the chamber') + درآیی (you enter / may you enter). The final clause به من ننگری uses the negative prefix ن- with the present-subjunctive/imperative نگرَی to forbid the action: 'do not look at me.' به before من marks the indirect object/target ('at me').
segments.s066-g37: دل روشنم is a nominal phrase 'my heart bright/clear' (روشن with pronominal suffix -م). بر تو شد بدگمان uses شد ('became') with the predicate بدگمان ('suspicious') and the prepositional phrase بر تو ('toward you') to mean 'became suspicious toward you.' The clause as a whole describes a change of emotional state.
segments.s066-g38: بگویی is a subjunctive/jussive form meaning 'you may say' or 'say' in reported/requested speech; را is the definite object marker attached after (here written as separate particle) and emphasises the object (mora -> 'to me'). تا introduces the content/result clause ('that' / 'whether'), and the final compressed poetic phrase زهی‌گر کمان functions as a figurative comparison with کمان ('bow'). In classical poetry such compact compounds are common; grammatically treat زهی‌گر + کمان as the predicate/content of what is to be said ('like the bow / as a bow-string').
segments.s066-g39: بدو گفت is an inversion where بدو ('to him') precedes گفت ('said') — a very common classical ordering: 'to him said'. زن من is 'my woman' / 'my wife' with the possessor following (classical placement), یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain', and چاره‌جوی is a compound noun 'seeker of a remedy' (i.e. someone trying to find a way).
segments.s066-g40: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle meaning 'always / continually' or marking habitual action. نان is the object 'bread' (here idiomatically 'livelihood/food'). فراز آرم is a verb phrase: فراز ('on high, up') + آرم (1st-person form) giving 'I bring up / I will bring up' — in classical Persian the present/subjunctive form can carry an intention or habitual sense. از چند روی is a prepositional phrase 'from several sides/sources' (چند 'several' + روی 'side/way').
segments.s066-g81: This is an S–V–O sequence in a poetic register: زمین (subject) + دید (past tense of 'to see') + رودابه (proper-name object without the modern object marker را). The conjunction و then links the next noun phrase; پشت پای is a compound (پشت 'back/behind' + پای 'foot/step') meaning the backward step or the act of stepping back.
segments.s066-g82: فرو ماند is a compound: فرو (down/away) + ماند (past of ماندن 'remain') = 'remained/froze (down or in place)'. از خشم مادر is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/because of the mother's anger', and به جای is a fixed locational phrase 'in place / rooted to the spot'.
segments.s066-g83: فرو ریخت combines فرو (down) with ریخت (here = 'poured/flowed') to mean 'poured down'. از دیدگان marks the source 'from the eyes'; آب مهر is a genitive compound 'the water of love' (i.e. tears born of love). The word order places the source/possessor before the noun in classical poetic style.
segments.s066-g84: به خون دو نرگس is an instrumental/qualitative phrase 'with the blood (colour) of two narcissi' — a metaphor for a flushed or narcissus‑like beauty; بیاراست is a past/predicative verb 'adorned' (bi‑arāst) and چهر is the object 'face'. Note the poetic omission of an object marker and the figurative use of خون ('blood' = redness).
segments.s066-g85: به مادر چنین گفت is a reporting clause: به مادر = 'to (her) mother' (indirect object), چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past tense 'said' (introducing direct speech). کای is the vocative particle 'O' and پر خرد is an epithet meaning 'full of wisdom' (پر = 'full of' + خرد = 'wisdom'), addressing the mother as 'O wise one.'
segments.s066-g86: In همی مهر جان مرا بشکرد, همی is an intensifying/adverbial particle ('indeed/this very' or 'ever'), مهر جان = 'love of the soul' (love as affecting the life), مرا + را (written here split as م + را in the segment list) is the object 'me/my', and بشکرد is an archaic past verb 'to break/shatter' — so the phrase means 'this very love has broken my life/soul.'
segments.s066-g87: مرا is the direct object pronoun 'me' (presented here before the vocative), مام فرخ is a vocative or epithet 'Mother Farrokh' (مام = mother; فرخ = 'fortunate/bright' as a name/epithet). نزادی is an archaic past of 'زادن' (to beget/give birth), and ز بن literally 'from the root' = 'of the stock/lineage' (i.e. 'of noble/rooted lineage'). The sequence reads as an address to the mother about the speaker's origin.
segments.s066-g88: نرفتی is a past negative form 'did not go/leave' used idiomatically here as 'did not refrain / did not spare'; ز من = 'about/of me', and نیک یا بد سخن = 'good or bad speech/words' (a coordinated phrase). Together this clause accuses the addressee of not holding back from speaking either praise or blame about the speaker.

sentences.s066-s61: Thus he saw Rūdābeh in secret, in a place where no one in the world would hear any counsel.
sentences.s066-s62: He came from his anguish weeping and lay down to sleep; still, only skin clung to his body — one would say it was enough.
sentences.s066-s49: A messenger was sent to the great (elder) Sam; he sent an answer to Zal the mighty.
sentences.s066-s50: After a while he turned, and there was an order; the proper words were said and heard.
sentences.s066-s51: He gave the messenger many things; I heard all of Sam’s reply as well.
sentences.s066-s52: By the hand of that very woman — the one who plucked out his hair — you struck it on the ground and dragged it across the face.
sentences.s066-s21: Rudabeh wanted decoration for this chamber; I gave it to her — that is exactly how it is now.
sentences.s066-s22: I fetched for her a richly-decorated helmet and a single jeweled, regal ring.
sentences.s066-s23: He said to her, 'Sindokht, show yourself to me; I am love-struck — free me from this thought.'
sentences.s066-s24: I entrusted them to Rudabeh; she said those two items she wanted in addition — bring them now as well.
sentences.s066-s9: He approached Rudabeh; like the wind, he brought her this joyful tidings.
sentences.s066-s10: The fairy-faced one showered dirhams upon the woman; she set her figure upon a golden seat.
sentences.s066-s11: A certain shining headband was brought forward; its warp and weft inside had become invisible.
sentences.s066-s12: Her whole form became ruby-red and golden; all the gold had disappeared from the jewel.
sentences.s066-s45: The commander remained in Kabul; thus he set his seal of love upon the fire.
sentences.s066-s46: It pressed so tightly on my heart and on the world that I wept, both openly and in secret.
sentences.s066-s47: I would not wish to be a living body without her face; my world is not worth a single hair of hers.
sentences.s066-s48: To the one who saw me and sat with me — to that man we pledged; we took his hand in our hand.
sentences.s066-s53: He was the messenger who brought the letter; this garment was the reply to me.
sentences.s066-s54: Sindukht was left (overcome) by that conversation; Zal found her pleasing and she became his match.
sentences.s066-s55: He answered thus: 'This is no wisdom; such tales are not (fit) among the nobles.'
sentences.s066-s56: The son of the mighty world‑champion is great; all his renown and every counsel of his are of a clear (bright) spirit.
sentences.s066-s57: He possessed every art, yet before that gazelle even skill seemed small.
sentences.s066-s58: The king of the world became so enraged; from Kabul he raised dust up to the sun.
sentences.s066-s59: He will not allow that from our seed on earth any ignoble person place a despised foot upon the saddle.
sentences.s066-s60: He released the woman and struck her; he made her appear so that he did not recognize her.
sentences.s066-s33: She washed the two flowers with two sleepy narcissi until the blossoms became dewy.
sentences.s066-s34: He said to Rudabeh, 'O lofty, moon-faced one, did you choose from the court's coquettish charms the well?'
sentences.s066-s35: What good was left of what you had in the world that I did not make manifest to you, openly and in secret?
sentences.s066-s36: Why have you become cruel/oppressive, O moon-faced one? Tell all the secrets before (to) your mother.
sentences.s066-s25: Baha said, "Let one drop of water be placed upon my eye — pour it upon this anger of mine."
sentences.s066-s26: He said, "Give the dirham tomorrow to the moon‑faced one, Baha; do not seek me until I find you."
sentences.s066-s27: He regarded his speech as crooked and made his heart ready for battle against him.
sentences.s066-s28: He came and sprang upon him, and swiftly leapt at him because of his crookedness and shortcomings.
sentences.s066-s1: Between the commander and that cypress‑trunk there was a woman, a sweet‑speaking teller of pleasant words.
sentences.s066-s2: You brought a message to the champion; likewise, from the champion you proceeded toward the cypress.
sentences.s066-s3: The commander recited my tales to her; whatever words he had heard he repeated to her.
sentences.s066-s4: He said to him, 'Go close to Rudabeh; tell her, “O kind‑hearted, new moon.”'
sentences.s066-s29: That woman was filled with inward anger and bore grief; she endured humiliation on the earth.
sentences.s066-s30: When she saw those rich garments, Rudabeh likewise saw the ornament slip from her hand.
sentences.s066-s31: Within the palace she wrapped herself up; losing her composure, she became in her conduct like a drunk.
sentences.s066-s32: He commanded that his daughter go forward; she then laid her hand upon her own cheek.
sentences.s066-s13: A single pair of rings, richly set with gems, shining like Jupiter on the firmament.
sentences.s066-s14: He sent it into Sam’s hands; he gave many greetings and that message.
sentences.s066-s15: The woman came from the chamber to the porch; then she paused, and Siandokht saw her.
sentences.s066-s16: The woman, out of fear, turned back; she was afraid of the sandalwood and kissed the ground.
sentences.s066-s37: Who is this woman who keeps coming from before, and why does she come before you?
sentences.s066-s38: What is the nature of this speech, and who is that man who is handsome, wearing a headband and a ring?
sentences.s066-s39: From the great treasure (came) the Tazians' helmet; to us there remained much gain and loss.
sentences.s066-s40: May, under this evil name, the seeking of justice be cast to the wind; may a daughter like me never be born.
sentences.s066-s5: When the speech, pressed by narrowness, reached hardship, you will soon see its breadth — the key.
sentences.s066-s6: The messenger came back from before Sam, with joyful and auspicious tidings.
sentences.s066-s7: He spoke much and listened, and told the tale; in the end he became of the same mind.
sentences.s066-s8: He handed over the woman's brief reply; the woman returned from his presence and took it away.
sentences.s066-s17: Sindokht's soul became full of thought about him; she said aloud, "Tell me, from where are you?"
sentences.s066-s18: From time to time pass before me; enter the chamber, but do not look at me.
sentences.s066-s19: My heart, which was bright toward you, became distrustful; tell me (so) — whether I am like a bow's string.
sentences.s066-s20: To him the woman said, 'I am a certain seeker of a remedy; I will (constantly) bring bread from several sources.'
sentences.s066-s41: When the earth saw Rūdāba, the mother's backward step froze in place from anger.
sentences.s066-s42: Tears of love poured down from her eyes; her face was adorned with the blood (redness) of two narcissi.
sentences.s066-s43: She said to her mother thus: 'O wise one, this very love has broken my life.'
sentences.s066-s44: O Mother Farrokh, you did not give me birth of noble stock; you did not spare me from speaking — good or ill.
